archetypes.verdict: Mechanistically elegant but workflow-hostile: we have no validated Cell Cinema latent manifold in FFPE or blood, no CRISPR-Cure-7 GMP construct, and no reimbursement code for “temporal synchronization” of in vivo editing. Off-label use would require compassionate-use INDs for each edit (7× regulatory friction), while standard-of-care sequential TKIs or ADCs impose far lower patient burden and are already reimbursed. The stochastic control threshold θ_i(p) is unverifiable in real time without serial liquid-biopsy single-cell multi-omics synced to a generative video model—currently science-project infrastructure. Until we can show a >50% DTP-frequency reduction in a longitudinal patient-matched xenograft with clinically feasible sampling, this remains a sophisticated timing algorithm in search of a drug.
archetypes.would_move_price: A pre-registered, patient-matched PDX time-series showing that CRISPR-Cure-7 delivered at the Cell Cinema–derived t* reduces persister frequency by ≥50% versus time-agnostic editing would shift the price sharply up; failure to beat standard-of-care sequential AKT/BRD4 inhibition in the same model would crater it.

archetypes.verdict: This paper bridges dynamic tumor state predictions with precise CRISPR timing, offering a novel kinetic framework to disrupt drug-tolerant persisters. The mathematically derived intervention windows address a critical bottleneck in metastatic cancer treatment, with falsifiable targets (e.g., >50% DTP suppression). However, reliance on hypothetical CRISPR-Cure-7 and Cell Cinema data introduces clinical viability uncertainty, while the complex optimal control model may face regulatory scrutiny. If experimental validation confirms efficacy, this approach could revolutionize metastatic oncology, but current reliance on unproven platforms limits immediate patient impact.
archetypes.would_move_price: A successful Phase I trial demonstrating >50% DTP reduction with the proposed timing strategy would shift the prediction-market price up; failure to validate the kinetic model in vivo would shift it down.

archetypes.verdict: As an FDA Regulator, I assess this paper with a focus on approval pathway, IND-enabling studies, safety signals, accelerated-approval eligibility, and prior precedents. The clinical viability score of 7 reflects reliance on hypothetical CRISPR-Cure-7 and Cell Cinema, which lack IND-enabling data (toxicity, efficacy, manufacturing) to support approval. The regulatory risk of 8 stems from unproven safety signals, complex stochastic optimal control models requiring validation, and no prior precedents for temporal synchronization in CRISPR therapies. Market potential is 9 due to targeting metastatic cancer—a high-need area with unmet needs—and patient impact is 10 because it addresses drug-tolerant persisters driving recurrence. Novelty is 9 for integrating stochastic optimal control with gene-editing timing, and falsifiability is 8 as it proposes testable hypotheses (e.g., >50% DTP reduction). Overall, the paper is highly novel and patient-impact-driven but faces significant regulatory hurdles due to missing IND data and unproven safety.
archetypes.would_move_price: A publication of IND-enabling studies showing >60% DTP suppression with no off-target effects in preclinical models would shift the prediction-market price up.

archetypes.verdict: The paper presents a mathematically rigorous framework for synchronizing CRISPR-Cure-7 with DTP commitment windows, offering a novel approach to disrupt metastatic cancer persistence. While the integration of Cell Cinema's latent trajectory modeling with optimal control theory is conceptually compelling, clinical viability remains uncertain due to the complexity of translating precise temporal synchronization into in vivo applications. Regulatory risk is elevated given the gene-editing nature of CRISPR-Cure-7, which faces stringent FDA scrutiny. Market potential is high for metastatic cancer therapies, but time-to-clinic is likely prolonged due to the need for validation of Cell Cinema's predictive accuracy and CRISPR timing in clinical settings. Patient impact is substantial if successful, targeting a critical unmet need. The falsifiability is strong, with clear hypotheses and measurable endpoints. Overall, the work advances mechanistic understanding but requires significant validation to de-risk.
archetypes.would_move_price: A successful preclinical validation of Cell Cinema's DTP commitment window predictions would sharply increase the price, while off-target editing effects in early trials could crash it.

archetypes.verdict: A conceptually elegant but clinically premature bridge between video-generative latent dynamics and multiplex CRISPR timing. The math is publishable (optimal control on a persister manifold) and will excite systems-biology crowds at AACR/CSHL, but oncologists will attack the absence of in vivo validation, undefined editing cargo, and a regulatory pathway that does not exist for synchronized multi-axis germline/somatic editing. It slots as a high-risk/high-reward methods paper rather than a therapeutic proposal; champions will cite the Jacobian-based vulnerability window, while adversaries will hammer the leap from Cell Cinema surrogates to dosing schedules.
archetypes.would_move_price: Publication of a longitudinal single-cell CRISPR screen showing that Cell Cinema–predicted t* indeed yields >50% reduction in DTP outgrowth would sharply move price up; failure to replicate the consensus window in an independent organoid cohort would crash it.

archetypes.verdict: This paper presents a theoretically rigorous framework for synchronizing CRISPR-Cure-7 with DTP commitment windows, which is methodologically impressive but remains unvalidated in real-world settings. From a patient advocate lens, the novelty lies in its kinetic modeling approach, which could theoretically reduce side-effect burden by targeting interventions precisely. However, clinical viability is low without empirical data on safety, accessibility, or patient-reported outcomes. Regulatory risk is high due to the complexity of integrating AI-driven timing models with gene-editing therapies. Patient impact is moderate—potential exists if synchronization reduces treatment duration or toxicity, but this requires validation. Falsifiability is strong via the proposed DTP frequency reduction endpoint. Overall, the work is scientifically bold but premature for direct patient benefit.
archetypes.would_move_price: A successful Phase I trial demonstrating reduced side-effect burden and improved patient-reported quality of life would sharply increase the prediction-market price.

headline_catalysts: [Practicing Oncologist] A pre-registered, patient-matched PDX time-series showing that CRISPR-Cure-7 delivered at the Cell Cinema–derived t* reduces persister frequency by ≥50% versus time-agnostic editing would shift the price sharply up; failure to beat standard-of-care sequential AKT/BRD4 inhibition in the same model would crater it.

[Biostatistician] A successful Phase I trial demonstrating >50% DTP reduction with the proposed timing strategy would shift the prediction-market price up; failure to validate the kinetic model in vivo would shift it down.
[FDA Regulator] A publication of IND-enabling studies showing >60% DTP suppression with no off-target effects in preclinical models would shift the prediction-market price up.

[Biotech Investor] A successful preclinical validation of Cell Cinema's DTP commitment window predictions would sharply increase the price, while off-target editing effects in early trials could crash it.

[Academic KOL] Publication of a longitudinal single-cell CRISPR screen showing that Cell Cinema–predicted t* indeed yields >50% reduction in DTP outgrowth would sharply move price up; failure to replicate the consensus window in an independent organoid cohort would crash it.

derived_topics.title: Stochastic Perturbation of Latent Manifold Bifurcation Points via CRISPR-Cure-7
derived_topics.rationale: Extends the deterministic t* derivation to account for stochasticity. Hypothesis: Applying edits at the bifurcation point of the latent flow maximizes the probability of driving cells into apoptosis rather than dormancy. Endpoint: Probability of state transition via single-cell trajectories.

derived_topics.title: Kinetic Jacobian Mapping of Axis-Specific Sensitivity (S_i) in Organoid Models
derived_topics.rationale: Moves from theoretical Jacobian derivation to empirical validation. Hypothesis: The measured sensitivity S_i(t) of the 7 axes in patient-derived organoids correlates with the Cell Cinema-predicted directional derivatives. Endpoint: Pearson correlation between predicted and measured S_i.

derived_topics.title: Asymmetric Multi-Axis Editing: Optimizing Non-Simultaneous CRISPR Delivery
derived_topics.rationale: Addresses the 'suboptimal simultaneous exposure' finding. Hypothesis: A staggered, time-indexed delivery of the 7 axes according to their individual t_i^peak maximizes fitness loss compared to bolus delivery. Endpoint: Fold-reduction in DTP outgrowth in longitudinal assays.

derived_topics.title: Off-Target Mitigation via Latent-Space Plasticity Constraints
derived_topics.rationale: Extends the objective function J(t*) to include a penalty for editing during high-plasticity/low-commitment windows. Hypothesis: Restricting edits to narrow commitment windows reduces off-target genomic instability in non-DTP cells. Endpoint: Frequency of non-target mutations in healthy cell controls.

derived_topics.title: Feedback-Loop Control: Real-Time Cell Cinema Updates for Adaptive CRISPR Dosing
derived_topics.rationale: Moves from open-loop to closed-loop control. Hypothesis: Real-time monitoring of latent trajectory shifts allows for mid-course correction of the CRISPR-Cure-7 schedule. Endpoint: Reduction in clonal escape rates in evolving tumor populations.

peer_reviews.summary: This paper presents a novel and ambitious approach to synchronizing CRISPR-Cure-7 with Cell Cinema-predicted DTP commitment windows, offering a sophisticated mathematical framework for optimal intervention timing. However, the complexity of the proposed models and the lack of experimental validation raise concerns about the feasibility and robustness of the approach. The paper would benefit from a more detailed explanation of the assumptions underlying the models and additional simulations or experiments to support the key claims.
criticisms: The paper lacks experimental data to support the proposed mathematical models.
The assumptions underlying the stochastic optimal control model and the Wasserstein distance framework are not fully justified.
The paper does not address potential confounders in the Cell Cinema predictions.
The sample size and power analysis for any potential experiments are not discussed.
required_fixes: Provide experimental data or simulations that validate the mathematical models.
Justify the assumptions made in the stochastic optimal control model and the Wasserstein distance framework.
Discuss potential confounders in the Cell Cinema predictions and how they might affect the results.
Include a sample size and power analysis for any proposed experiments.
suggested_experiments: Conduct in vitro experiments to test the predicted optimal intervention windows for CRISPR-Cure-7.
Perform in vivo studies to validate the efficacy of synchronized CRISPR-Cure-7 interventions in reducing DTP emergence.
Compare the performance of stochastic synchronization versus fixed-timing strategies in reducing DTP emergence.

simulation_proposal.description: Develop a computational model that simulates the dynamics of DTP commitment and the impact of CRISPR-Cure-7 interventions at various time points. This model should incorporate the stochastic nature of DTP commitment and the kinetic dependencies of the seven resistance axes.
simulation_proposal.rationale: This simulation will provide a controlled environment to test the hypotheses generated by the mathematical models and identify the optimal intervention windows without the complexities of in vivo experiments.

peer_reviews.summary: This paper presents purely theoretical mathematical frameworks for synchronizing a hypothetical gene-editing strategy (CRISPR-Cure-7) with predicted drug-tolerant persister commitment windows using an equally hypothetical platform (Cell Cinema). From an FDA regulatory lens, the manuscript lacks any IND-enabling data, safety assessments, or experimental validation. The mathematical derivations, while computationally interesting, rest on unvalidated assumptions about latent manifold structure, pathway sensitivity functions, and DTP commitment dynamics. No in vitro, in vivo, or even in silico validation data are presented. The claims of falsifiability are hollow without concrete experimental predictions. This work is premature for publication and requires fundamental experimental groundwork before theoretical extension.
required_fixes: Provide experimental validation of at least one mathematical prediction (e.g., timing-dependent CRISPR editing efficacy)
Establish existence and characterization of CRISPR-Cure-7 and Cell Cinema platforms with published data
Include safety assessment data from cellular or animal models
Define measurable endpoints that could support accelerated-approval pathway
Provide IND-enabling study roadmap with timelines
Include toxicology data or at least a safety pharmacology framework
Validate the latent space embedding and Jacobian approximations with real single-cell data
Test the optimal intervention time formula against experimental DTP frequency measurements
Establish delivery vector characterization and tissue-specific targeting data
Define the biomarker strategy for patient selection
suggested_experiments: Validate Cell Cinema latent trajectory predictions against longitudinal single-cell RNA-seq of drug-treated cancer cell lines
Test CRISPR-Cure-7 editing efficacy at multiple timepoints post-drug treatment to generate empirical S_i(t) curves
Measure DTP frequency at predicted optimal t* versus time-agnostic dosing in xenograft models
Conduct dose-escalation toxicology studies in relevant animal models
Develop and validate a biomarker assay for p(t) commitment probability measurement
Test the Wasserstein distance threshold prediction empirically in vitro

simulation_proposal.description: Agent-based simulation of tumor cell populations undergoing drug treatment with time-stochastic CRISPR editing interventions. Model parameters (S_i(t), λ_i, w_i) would be fit to preliminary experimental data from cell line DTP assays, then used to compare predicted DTP frequencies at mathematically optimized t* versus fixed-interval dosing.
expected_metrics: DTP frequency reduction at optimal t* vs control
Fold-change in clonal expansion over 28-day simulation
Off-target mutation rate as function of editing multiplicity
Robustness of t* prediction to parameter uncertainty (Monte Carlo sensitivity analysis)
simulation_proposal.rationale: This in silico validation is the minimum necessary step before animal studies - it would test whether the mathematical optimization actually produces the predicted therapeutic gain, and identify parameter sensitivities that could inform experimental design. Without this, the mathematical frameworks are unfalsifiable speculation.

peer_reviews.summary: This paper presents a novel and mechanistically interesting approach to optimizing the temporal synchronization of CRISPR-Cure-7 with Cell Cinema-predicted DTP commitment windows. The mathematical framework is robust and the integration of stochastic optimal control with topological optimization is innovative. However, the paper would benefit from additional clarity in the experimental design section and more explicit discussion of potential failure modes. The suggested experiments and simulations could strengthen the overall impact of the study.
criticisms: The paper lacks a clear experimental validation section, which is crucial for assessing the practical applicability of the proposed framework.
The discussion of failure modes is somewhat limited, particularly in the context of real-world biological variability.
The paper could benefit from more explicit connections to existing literature on DTP programs and CRISPR-based therapies.
required_fixes: Include a section detailing the experimental validation of the proposed synchronization strategy, with specific protocols and expected outcomes.
Expand the discussion of failure modes, including potential sources of error in the latent space predictions and how they might affect the intervention strategy.
Provide a more thorough literature review to contextualize the study within the broader field of cancer research and gene-editing therapies.
suggested_experiments: Conduct in vitro experiments using cancer cell lines to validate the predicted optimal intervention windows for CRISPR-Cure-7.
Perform in vivo studies in mouse models of metastatic cancer to assess the efficacy of the synchronized editing strategy in reducing DTP emergence.
Compare the outcomes of the synchronized editing strategy with a control group receiving time-agnostic CRISPR edits to quantify the therapeutic gain.

simulation_proposal.description: Develop a computational model that simulates the dynamics of DTP commitment and the impact of CRISPR-Cure-7 edits at various time points. This model should incorporate stochastic fluctuations in the latent trajectory and predict the distribution of admissible windows for intervention.
simulation_proposal.rationale: This simulation will provide a quantitative assessment of the proposed synchronization strategy and help identify the most effective intervention windows, thereby strengthening the theoretical framework presented in the paper.

peer_reviews.summary: The manuscript proposes an intriguing mathematical framework for synchronizing a hypothetical multi‑pathway CRISPR strategy with predicted drug‑tolerant persister windows, but it lacks empirical grounding, clear experimental validation plans, and sufficient detail to assess feasibility from a patient‑advocate perspective. Major revisions are required to address methodological gaps, clarify clinical relevance, and provide concrete evidence or simulation results.
criticisms: 1. The core hypothesis that a single optimal editing time (t*) exists for seven distinct pathways is mathematically derived but never tested in vitro or in vivo, leaving the claim unsubstantiated.

2. The model assumes perfect knowledge of latent trajectories and instantaneous editing efficacy, ignoring practical constraints such as delivery kinetics, editing lag, and off‑target effects that patients would experience.
3. Patient‑relevant outcomes (e.g., toxicity, quality of life, treatment burden) are not addressed; the manuscript focuses solely on theoretical fitness gains.
4. The discussion of stochastic versus fixed‑timing strategies is incomplete and lacks quantitative benchmarks or sensitivity analyses.
5. Key parameters (w_i, λ_i, τ_i) are arbitrarily chosen or calibrated to hypothetical data, with no reference to real omics datasets or clinical measurements.
6. The manuscript cites “CancerHawk Block 1/2/3” without providing accessible data or code, making reproducibility impossible.
7. The use of advanced mathematical constructs (Wasserstein distance, Dirichlet processes) is not justified by empirical evidence and may obscure rather than clarify the biological problem.
8. No patient‑reported outcome measures or real‑world evidence are incorporated, limiting the translational impact.
9. The manuscript contains numerous typographical and formatting errors that hinder comprehension of the equations and narrative.
10. The abstract and introduction overstate the clinical readiness of the approach, potentially misleading readers about the stage of development.
required_fixes: 1. Provide a detailed experimental plan (e.g., single‑cell CRISPR editing with time‑course sampling) that tests the predicted t* and demonstrates a >50% reduction in DTP frequency.
2. Include data or simulations showing the impact of editing latency, delivery efficiency, and off‑target activity on the optimal timing.
3. Incorporate patient‑centric endpoints: estimate toxicity burden, dosing frequency, and potential impact on quality of life using realistic delivery modalities.
4. Perform sensitivity analyses on w_i, λ_i, τ_i, and show robustness of t* to parameter perturbations.
5. Validate the latent trajectory predictions with an independent single‑cell RNA‑seq dataset and quantify prediction uncertainty (e.g., Brier score).
6. Add a section on clinical feasibility, discussing delivery vectors, immune response, and regulatory considerations.
7. Revise the manuscript for clarity: correct equations, improve figure legends, and ensure consistent terminology.
8. Provide access to the Cell Cinema latent model and CRISPR‑Cure‑7 design specifications (code, data) to enable reproducibility.
9. Discuss how the proposed strategy could be integrated into existing treatment regimens and what real‑world barriers might arise.
10. Cite relevant literature on DTP biology, CRISPR delivery, and temporal therapy optimization to contextualize the work.
suggested_experiments: 1. Perform a time‑resolved CRISPR‑Cas9 editing experiment in a metastatic cancer cell line, delivering individual pathway edits at predicted t* versus random times, and quantify persister emergence via single‑cell viability assays.
2. Use live‑cell imaging of a dormancy marker (e.g., p27^Kip1) to validate the latent trajectory predictions and confirm that editing at t* precedes commitment.
3. Conduct a dose‑response study to measure off‑target editing rates at different delivery times, correlating with the predicted λ_i decay curves.
4. Implement a patient‑derived xenograft (PDX) model to assess the impact of the optimized editing schedule on tumor regrowth and systemic toxicity.
5. Survey patients undergoing gene‑editing trials to capture real‑world treatment burden and side‑effect profiles, comparing fixed‑timing versus adaptive scheduling.

simulation_proposal.description: Build a stochastic agent‑based simulation of tumor cell populations incorporating the latent manifold dynamics, CRISPR editing kinetics, and off‑target effects. The model will generate synthetic single‑cell trajectories under varying editing schedules to test whether the analytically derived t* consistently yields the lowest persister frequency.
expected_metrics: Reduction in persister frequency compared to baseline
Sensitivity of t* to parameter noise
simulation_proposal.rationale: A simulation will bridge the gap between the elegant mathematical derivation and biological reality, allowing systematic exploration of uncertainties, delivery constraints, and patient‑relevant outcomes before costly wet‑lab validation.

peer_reviews.summary: This paper presents a novel approach to optimizing the temporal synchronization of CRISPR-Cure-7 with Cell Cinema-predicted DTP commitment windows, offering a sophisticated mathematical framework that could significantly enhance the efficacy of gene-editing therapies. However, the paper requires major revisions to clarify several complex mathematical derivations, to provide more concrete experimental evidence, and to address potential failure modes more thoroughly. The integration of stochastic optimal control with topological optimization and Bayesian causal modeling is innovative but needs further validation and simplification for broader understanding.
criticisms: The paper lacks concrete experimental data to support the proposed mathematical models, relying heavily on theoretical frameworks.
The complexity of the mathematical derivations may be a barrier for readers who are not experts in optimal control theory and topological optimization.
The failure modes discussed are not sufficiently explored, especially in terms of their impact on the overall efficacy of the proposed strategy.
The paper does not adequately address the cost-effectiveness ratio and ICER thresholds, which are crucial for insurance payers.
required_fixes: Provide concrete experimental data or simulations that validate the proposed mathematical models.
Simplify the mathematical derivations and provide more intuitive explanations for non-expert readers.
Expand the discussion on failure modes and their potential impact on therapeutic outcomes.
Include an analysis of the cost-effectiveness ratio and ICER thresholds to justify the potential coverage policy.
suggested_experiments: Conduct in vitro experiments to test the predicted optimal intervention windows for CRISPR-Cure-7.
Perform in vivo studies to validate the reduction in DTP emergence when edits are applied at the derived optimal time.
Assess the budget impact of implementing this synchronized editing strategy compared to current standard therapies.

simulation_proposal.description: Develop a computational model that simulates the stochastic dynamics of DTP commitment and the application of CRISPR-Cure-7 edits at various time points. This model should incorporate the mathematical frameworks proposed in the paper and allow for the testing of different intervention strategies.
simulation_proposal.rationale: This simulation will provide a controlled environment to test the efficacy of the proposed synchronization strategy and identify the optimal intervention windows without the need for extensive experimental trials.

peer_reviews.summary: This paper constructs an elegant mathematical framework for synchronizing CRISPR-Cure-7 with predicted DTP commitment windows, but it collapses at the first empirical hinge: neither CRISPR-Cure-7 nor Cell Cinema’s latent-space commitment windows have been experimentally validated in any model system, rendering the entire synchronization hypothesis unfalsifiable in practice. The proposed optimal control law and Wasserstein-based commitment metric depend on real-time estimation of p(t)—a latent DTP posterior probability—yet no assay exists to measure p(t) with sufficient temporal resolution or specificity to trigger interventions within the predicted narrow windows (±1–2 h). Without a ground-truth readout of commitment state, the model is a self-consistent fiction, not a testable hypothesis.
criticisms: CRISPR-Cure-7 is described as a 'hypothetical multi-pathway gene-editing strategy developed in CancerHawk Block 2'—a non-existent, internally referenced construct with no public validation or independent replication [Section 1].

Cell Cinema's latent coordinates φ_i(t) are claimed to predict DTP commitment windows, but no ground-truth benchmark (e.g., live imaging of dormancy exit or long-term repopulation after synchronized perturbation) is provided to validate the temporal accuracy of these predictions [Section 2].

The key metric p(t), the 'posterior probability of DTP commitment,' is introduced in Section 3 but never defined operationally; there is no assay that maps to p(t), making the Pontryagin-based threshold rule θ_i(p) = Φ⁻¹(1−τ_i) experimentally unimplementable [Section 3, first framework].

The Wasserstein manifold alignment assumes a known DTP-indicator manifold M_DTP, yet M_DTP is constructed from 'single-cell trajectories of cells that have irreversibly entered dormancy'—a circular definition, as dormancy irreversibility itself requires longitudinal functional validation that is absent [Section 3, second framework].

The paper claims stochastic synchronization outperforms fixed timing when σ_ε > 0.25, but the Brier score σ_ε is never measured or calibrated to any experimental outcome—only simulated [Section 3, third framework].
required_fixes: Provide experimental validation of CRISPR-Cure-7 efficacy (e.g., in vitro DTP suppression in at least two independent models) prior to proposing temporal synchronization.

Demonstrate that Cell Cinema can predict *functional* DTP commitment windows—e.g., by showing that perturbation within predicted windows reduces long-term regrowth by ≥50% versus off-window perturbation, with error bars from replicates.
Define p(t) in terms of measurable biomarkers (e.g., live reporters for quiescence, DNA content, or mitochondrial membrane potential) and provide calibration curves linking these to p(t).

Replace the circular construction of M_DTP with an independent functional definition (e.g., 'cells that fail to re-enter cycle after 14 days post-washout of drug X') and validate manifold alignment against this ground truth.
Report empirical Brier scores from prospective predictions on held-out trajectories, not just Monte Carlo simulations.
suggested_experiments: Perform time-lapse imaging of a DTP reporter (e.g., NR2F2-mCherry + H2B-GFP) in response to transient drug pulse, then perturb at predicted t* using inducible CRISPR-Cas9 (e.g., 4OHT-inducible) targeting one axis of CRISPR-Cure-7 and measure persister frequency at day 14.

Generate synthetic latent trajectories via perturb-seq under controlled drug exposure and test whether Wasserstein proximity to M_DTP (defined by functional dormancy) predicts actual regrowth capacity.

Calibrate p(t) using a Bayesian state-estimation pipeline trained on live-cell imaging of commitment markers (e.g., p27, FOXO3a localization, ROS), and test whether real-time triggering of editing at p(t) > θ improves DTP suppression versus fixed timing.

simulation_proposal.description: Simulate a stochastic DTP commitment process using a 5-dimensional latent ODE model (e.g., extending the ODE in Section 3 with noise terms) where true p(t) is known, then inject Cell Cinema-like observational noise (dropout, batch, temporal jitter) to test whether the proposed Wasserstein or Pontryagin-based t* estimation recovers the optimal intervention time with <15% error across 1000 trials.
expected_metrics: DTP suppression vs. ground-truth optimal timing
failure rate when latent manifold mis-specified by >10%
simulation_proposal.rationale: This directly tests whether the mathematical machinery survives realistic noise levels and model misspecification—without which the synchronization hypothesis collapses in vivo.

simulations.title: Tumor Volume (3D)
simulations.description: Volumetric point-cloud tumor with phenotype-coloured clusters that grow, divide, and apoptose over time. Rotates the mass to expose where the model places phenotype boundaries.
simulations.rationale: The 2D manifold view collapses depth — a 3D volumetric rendering tests whether claimed phenotype clusters survive when seen from arbitrary angles.

parameters.title: Optimal Temporal Synchronization of CRISPR-Cure-7 with Cell Cinema-Predicted DTP Commitment Windows
parameters.critique: The paper lacks experimental data to support the proposed mathematical models.
parameters.catalyst: [Practicing Oncologist] A pre-registered, patient-matched PDX time-series showing that CRISPR-Cure-7 delivered at the Cell Cinema–derived t* reduces persister frequency by ≥50% versus time-agnostic editing would shift the price sharply up; failure to beat standard-of-care sequential AKT/BRD4 inhibition in the same model would crater it.

simulations.title: Mitotic Lattice (3D)
simulations.description: Rotating 3D lattice of cells with division events sampled from the validator-emitted novelty distribution. Daughter cells inherit colour from parent phenotype, exposing lineage drift.
simulations.rationale: Hooks the falsifier visually: if the paper's mechanism predicts a specific division geometry, this scene makes deviations from it immediately visible.

parameters.title: Optimal Temporal Synchronization of CRISPR-Cure-7 with Cell Cinema-Predicted DTP Commitment Windows
parameters.critique: The paper lacks experimental data to support the proposed mathematical models.
parameters.catalyst: [Biostatistician] A successful Phase I trial demonstrating >50% DTP reduction with the proposed timing strategy would shift the prediction-market price up; failure to validate the kinetic model in vivo would shift it down.

simulations.title: Perturbation Cone (3D)
simulations.description: 3D analog of the counterfactual scene: treated trajectories spread out from a baseline manifold along a confidence-weighted cone. Cone width tracks acceptance probability.
simulations.rationale: Forces the prediction-vs-observation gap into a single visual. A narrow, well-aimed cone implies confident divergence; a fat cone signals high predictive uncertainty.
